title: Population firing rate over time

purpose.canonicalQuestion: How does the event rate of a declared population change over time, using auditable raw counts and an explicitly declared denominator?
cannotEstablish: That the recorded senders are representative of any larger population.
That a rate change was caused by any particular input, manipulation, or mechanism.
The rate of neurons that were not recorded. A rate is computed over the DECLARED recorded universe and says nothing about anything outside it.

An instantaneous or kernel-smoothed rate. Revision 2 accepts literal event-count bins only; a kernel branch remains structurally absent until its executable kernel, edge policy, table, summary, legend and geometry exist together.

normalization: Recorder output is NOT assumed to be chronological. Events are stably sorted by (time, senderId, original ordinal) for analysis. The embedded canonical request and its input digest bind the source event arrays, but revision 2 emits no separate original-order digest.
The observation window is half-open [start, stop) by default. An event exactly at `stop` is EXCLUDED, and the count of such excluded events is reported rather than silently absorbed.

Bins tile the exact half-open window [start, stop). Their final edge is EXCLUSIVE: an event exactly at stop is outside the observation window and cannot be rescued by a different histogram convention.
Duplicate identical event rows are PRESERVED. Two records of the same event are a source-data question, not something the renderer may quietly resolve.

The denominator is the number of RECORDED senders. It is never the number of senders that happened to spike: a neuron that stayed silent was still recorded, and treating it as absent inflates every per-neuron rate — often dramatically, and always in the flattering direction.
derivation: binned_count: for each half-open bin, count the events whose time falls in it. Counts are exact non-negative integers.

mean_rate_per_recorded_sender: rate = count / (recordedSenderCount * binWidth_in_seconds). The bin width is converted to seconds through the typed unit layer, never through a hard-coded factor of 1000.
total_event_rate: rate = count / binWidth_in_seconds. This carries NO per-neuron claim and is the correct choice when the recorded universe is unknown.

When the caller supplies pre-binned rows containing BOTH a raw count and a rate, the rate is re-derived from the count and the denominator and checked within a tight binary64 tolerance. Cortexel re-computes rather than trusts.

Pre-binned conservation is independently checkable inside the request: sum(counts) must equal sourceEventCount exactly, recordedSenderCount must equal the complete unique recordedSenderIds length, and every optional rate must equal count divided by the declared exposure. These equalities establish internal consistency, not source authenticity.
Complexity: O(n log b + b) for exact declared-unit membership over n events and b bins; no event-order sort is required because counts are permutation-invariant.
scopeRules: The recorded-sender universe must be declared and non-empty.
Every event's sender must be a member of that universe: an event from a sender that was supposedly not recorded is a contradiction, not a rounding error.

recordedSenderIds.description: The COMPLETE set of senders that were recorded — including any that never fired. This is the denominator basis and it cannot be inferred from the events.

counts.description: Exact integer event counts per bin. Length must be edges.length - 1.

sourceEventCount.description: Exact number of in-window source events before binning. sum(counts) must equal it, so deletion of a first, middle or final bin is detectable. This is a caller-declared upstream fact whose internal consistency Cortexel checks; it is not a source-authenticity attestation.

recordedSenderIds.description: The complete recorded-sender universe, including silent senders. recordedSenderCount must equal its unique cardinality.

rates.description: Optional. If supplied it is RE-DERIVED from the counts and denominator and checked; it is never taken on trust.

rateMode.description: Revision 2 accepts literal event-count bins only. Kernel estimates remain structurally absent until their full scientific and rendering obligations are implemented.

normalization.description: mean_rate_per_recorded_sender divides by the recorded-sender count and IS a per-neuron claim. total_event_rate does not and is NOT. When the recorded universe is unknown, the honest choice is total_event_rate.

uncertainty.description: Explicit absence only. Revision 2 has no accepted dispersion or interval branch because no uncertainty kind yet reaches the table, summary, legend and geometry together.

accessibility.summaryTemplate: Population firing rate for {populationLabel} over the exact half-open window {windowStart} to {windowStop} {timeUnit}. {binCount} literal bins contain {eventCount} events from {recordedSenderCount} recorded senders, including silent senders. Normalization: {normalization}. Rate ranges from {rateMin} to {rateMax} Hz. No sampling uncertainty was supplied or rendered.

tableColumns.description: Exclusive upper edge, including the final bin.
Exact integer count. This is the raw observation everything else is derived from.
The denominator. Includes senders that never fired.
Derived and verified from the count and the denominator.

externalOracle.name: elephant.statistics.time_histogram
externalOracle.notes: Elephant's time_histogram is the intended differential oracle. Its bin edge and t_stop conventions must be matched parameter for parameter before the comparison means anything. The pinned reference environment has not been executed in this environment, so the status is not_run rather than assumed.

adapters.notes: Events plus the recorded sender universe. The adapter must supply the RECORDED senders, which NEST knows and the event list does not.
t_start/t_stop map to the window; the unit is taken from the quantity, never guessed.
The caller must select the unit rows explicitly.

migration.notes: The legacy payload did not distinguish recorded senders from senders that fired. Migration therefore REQUIRES a recorded-sender count and blocks without one rather than inferring the denominator from the events — which would have silently inflated every rate.

changePolicy: Changing the default bin boundary convention is MAJOR.
Changing the rate formula or its denominator is MAJOR.
Changing the meaning of `mean_rate_per_recorded_sender` is MAJOR.
Adding a fully executable kernel branch is MINOR only if it remains a tagged union branch with distinct table, summary, legend and geometry semantics; weakening binned-count meaning is MAJOR.
Adding a supported uncertainty variant is MINOR only when its method, denominator, table, summary, legend and geometry ship together.
knownLimitations: Kernel-smoothed rates are intentionally unsupported in revision 2. Cortexel does not accept a bandwidth it cannot execute and surface completely.

A rate is only as meaningful as the declared recorded universe. Cortexel can verify that the denominator is positive and that events belong to it; it cannot verify that the universe was recorded correctly.

invalid.why: The recorded-sender universe is absent, so a per-neuron rate has no denominator. Cortexel refuses rather than counting the senders that happened to spike — which would report a rate 33% too high here, since sender 4 never fired.

The supplied rate does not follow from the count and the denominator. 5 events / (4 senders x 0.005 s) is 250 Hz, not 999 Hz. Cortexel re-derives and rejects rather than drawing the number it was handed.

An event's sender is not in the declared recorded universe. A sender that was supposedly not recorded cannot have produced a recorded event; this is a contradiction in the source, not a value to silently include.

`ms` is canonical but `milliseconds` is only an alias. A stable request must use the canonical code; the alias is rejected with a repair rather than silently converted, because a silent conversion changes a number the caller never sees.
The request tries to assert that Cortexel validated it. Validation results are library-generated facts and are not caller-settable; the field is rejected, not ignored.
